proposals.title: Quantitative mapping of synaptic surface receptor proteomes to functional conductance identifies ΔFosB-regulated determinants of plasticity in D1-MSNs
proposals.motivation: Chronic exposure to drugs of abuse, like cocaine, induces persistent transcriptional adaptations such as ΔFosB accumulation in nucleus accumbens (NAc) D1-type medium spiny neurons (MSNs), driving long-lasting synaptic and behavioral plasticity. However, a critical gap exists between measuring total protein expression changes (e.g., via neuroproteomics) and understanding their functional impact on synaptic transmission. The precise relationship between the abundance and post-translational state of synaptic surface receptors and the resultant synaptic conductance remains poorly quantified, limiting predictive models of circuit adaptation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that cocaine-induced ΔFosB in D1-MSNs remodels synaptic strength by selectively altering the surface abundance and phosphorylation state of specific glutamate receptor subunits (e.g., GluA1, GluN2B), and that these molecular changes will quantitatively predict functional conductance measured at the same synapse.
proposals.method: We will employ a dual-approach system in primary cultured NAc D1-MSNs, identified by Drd1a-tdTomato fluorescence. First, we will use surface biotinylation followed by liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS) with isobaric tandem mass tag (TMT) labeling to obtain absolute quantitation of synaptic surface receptor copy number and phosphorylation states. Second, at the same identified synapses, we will perform simultaneous dual-patch clamp electrophysiology and quantitative live-cell imaging of pHluorin-tagged receptors to measure AMPA/NMDA receptor conductance and trafficking dynamics. Data integration and predictive modeling will be achieved using multivariate linear regression and random forest algorithms to correlate molecular profiles with functional outputs.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a first-of-its-kind quantitative map linking the synaptic surface proteome to functional conductance, moving beyond correlation to causation. It will identify the specific receptor pools and post-translational modifications that are the primary drivers of ΔFosB-mediated synaptic plasticity, providing a mechanistic, predictive framework for understanding addiction-related circuit adaptations.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct, synapse-specific correlation of absolute surface receptor quantitation with real-time electrophysiology, creating a predictive biophysical model. This overcomes the limitation of bulk tissue analyses and bridges the gap between molecular neuroproteomics and functional neurophysiology in a defined neuronal population central to reward pathology.

proposals.title: Quantifying Synaptically Available Glutamate Receptors in D1-MSNs with Fluorescent Ligand Analogs to Decipher Cocaine-Induced Neuroadaptations
proposals.motivation: A core challenge in addiction neuroscience is linking molecular changes, like ΔFosB accumulation in nucleus accumbens D1-MSNs, to functional synaptic alterations underlying persistent behavioral plasticity. Standard immunoblotting measures total receptor protein but cannot distinguish the functional, surface-exposed pool available for neurotransmission, creating a critical gap between proteomic data and circuit function. This is analogous to the clinical distinction between post-mortem receptor density and in vivo PET scans of available receptors.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that chronic cocaine exposure induces a mismatch between total and synaptically available AMPA and NMDA receptor subunits in D1-MSNs, and that this functional receptor pool, measurable with conformation-specific ligands, correlates more strongly with ΔFosB-mediated changes in neuronal excitability and connectivity than total protein levels.
proposals.method: We will develop cell-permeable, fluorescent analogs of established glutamate receptor antagonists (e.g., derivatives of NBQX for AMPARs, MK-801 for NMDARs) designed to bind specifically to the active-state conformation of surface receptors in live neurons. Using ex vivo brain slices from Fosb-TRAP or D1-Cre mice subjected to cocaine or saline self-administration, we will perform quantitative confocal imaging in identified D1-MSNs in the NAc core. This will be combined with electrophysiology to correlate ligand binding with synaptic currents, and with viral-mediated ΔFosB manipulation to establish causality.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel method to directly quantify the functional synaptic receptor landscape in defined cell types, moving beyond total protein assays. It will provide a direct, functional link between transcription factor-driven gene regulation (ΔFosB/CREB) and the resulting synaptic proteome, offering a mechanistic explanation for cocaine-induced changes in whole-brain functional connectivity originating in D1-MSNs.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel by adapting the core principle of PET radioligand binding—quantifying only the available, bindable receptor pool—to the cellular/circuit level in live brain tissue, using chemical rather than genetic probes to report on receptor conformational state and synaptic localization.

proposals.title: Cell-Type-Specific Synaptic AMPAR Trafficking and Conductance Deficits Underlie ΔFosB-Mediated Cocaine-Induced Plasticity
proposals.motivation: Chronic cocaine exposure induces stable ΔFosB accumulation in D1-MSNs of the NAc, driving persistent transcriptional adaptations linked to addiction. However, standard immunoblotting measures of total glutamate receptor subunits (e.g., GluA1/2) in tissue homogenates cannot distinguish between intracellular pools and functionally available synaptic surface receptors, creating a critical gap in understanding the true synaptic pathophysiology. This disconnect may explain why molecular changes measured biochemically often poorly correlate with electrophysiological and behavioral outcomes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that chronic cocaine-induced ΔFosB in D1-MSNs drives a specific redistribution of AMPAR subunits from the synaptic surface to intracellular compartments, and that this trafficking deficit, not the total protein level, underlies the altered synaptic strength and behavioral sensitization.
proposals.method: We will combine cell-type-specific neuroproteomics of synaptoneurosomes from NAc D1-MSNs (using FACS sorting from D1-Cre::RiboTag mice) with electrophysiological and super-resolution imaging. Specifically, we will use surface biotinylation assays followed by mass spectrometry to quantify synaptic surface vs. total receptor pools. Concurrently, we will perform patch-clamp recordings of AMPAR/NMDAR ratios and paired-pulse ratios in identified D1-MSNs in ex vivo slices from cocaine-treated mice. Data will be integrated using multivariate regression models to correlate ΔFosB levels, surface receptor abundance, and synaptic conductance.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a direct, quantitative link between a transcription factor (ΔFosB), its cell-type-specific synaptic proteomic targets, and functional electrophysiological readouts, moving beyond correlative total protein measures. It will define a precise synaptic mechanism for cocaine-induced plasticity and validate surface proteomics as a critical method for studying neuropsychiatric disease models.
proposals.novelty: While ΔFosB's role in addiction is known, its cell-type-specific effect on the spatial distribution (surface vs. intracellular) of synaptic proteins is uncharacterized. Integrating surface-specific proteomics with physiology in a defined neuronal population to dissect this discrepancy is a novel approach.

proposals.title: Resolving Methodological Blind Spots: A Multi-Modal Investigation of Region-Specific and Synaptically-Tethered Neuroadaptations in the Mesolimbic Circuit Following Cocaine Exposure
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 studies, including from our lab, often report VTA-selective molecular changes (e.g., in ΔFosB or CREB) after cocaine exposure, with null findings in the NAc or SN. This regional selectivity is a cornerstone of addiction models but may reflect methodological limitations. Standard immunoblotting lacks sensitivity for low-abundance proteins in small nuclei and cannot detect activity-dependent receptor trafficking to synaptic surfaces, a critical mechanism of neural plasticity.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that cocaine-induced neuroadaptations are not exclusive to the VTA but are present in the NAc and SN as well, manifesting primarily as changes in the synaptic localization and function of key receptors (e.g., dopamine, glutamate) rather than solely in total protein levels, which standard assays fail to capture.
proposals.method: We will use a convergent multi-modal approach in mice. First, we will employ cell-type-specific translating ribosome affinity purification (TRAP) from D1- and D2-MSNs in the NAc and dopamine neurons in the VTA/SN to sensitively quantify activity-regulated translatomes. Second, we will use surface biotinylation assays combined with quantitative proteomics to specifically isolate and measure synaptic membrane pools of receptors in each region. Third, we will validate functional consequences using ex vivo slice electrophysiology to measure AMPA/NMDA ratios and dopamine receptor sensitivity.
proposals.contribution: This work will challenge the paradigm of VTA-centric neuroadaptation by providing a higher-resolution map of cocaine-induced plasticity across the mesolimbic circuit. It will establish a critical methodological framework for distinguishing between total protein expression and functionally relevant, synaptically-tethered pools, directly testing the true negative in key regions.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct, simultaneous test of two pervasive methodological blind spots—regional detection limits and subcellular receptor localization—within the same experimental framework, moving beyond correlative protein measures to define the functionally engaged molecular substrate.

proposals.title: Causal role of ΔFosB in D1-MSNs of the nucleus accumbens as a convergent hub for stress- and cocaine-induced behavioral persistence
proposals.motivation: Chronic stress and cocaine exposure induce similar maladaptive behavioral states, such as increased avoidance and compulsive persistence, and both upregulate ΔFosB in the nucleus accumbens (NAc). While correlative, it is unknown if this molecular convergence in D1-type medium spiny neurons (D1-MSNs) represents a critical, shared mechanistic node or a circuit-specific epiphenomenon. Establishing causality is essential for understanding the core molecular logic of psychiatric comorbidity.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that ΔFosB induction in NAc D1-MSNs is a causally necessary molecular adaptation for the expression of persistent avoidance behavior, regardless of whether the inducing stimulus is chronic social defeat stress or chronic cocaine exposure.
proposals.method: We will use Cre-dependent AAV vectors in Drd1a-Cre mice to bidirectionally manipulate ΔFosB (overexpression vs. dominant-negative inhibition) selectively in NAc D1-MSNs. Mice will undergo either chronic social defeat stress or chronic cocaine administration, followed by behavioral assessment of persistence/avoidance (e.g., progressive ratio responding, active avoidance). Crucially, we will employ a cross-sensitization design: blocking ΔFosB in one pathway (e.g., after stress) and testing if the behavioral phenotype induced by the other stimulus (cocaine) is spared, using viral-mediated functional disconnection of specific afferent inputs (BNST→NAc for stress, BLA→NAc for cocaine).
proposals.contribution: This work will move beyond correlation to definitively test if ΔFosB in a single cell type is a genuine mechanistic hub for convergent pathology. It will establish a causal framework for understanding how distinct etiologies produce similar behavioral outcomes and identify a high-value target for intervention in comorbid addiction and depression.
proposals.novelty: While ΔFosB's role in drug addiction is well-studied, its causal necessity in stress-induced behaviors within the same neuronal population, and the use of an adversarial cross-sensitization design to dissect convergent mechanism, is a novel and critical test of the molecular hub hypothesis.

proposals.title: Divergent Circuit-Logic Drives Convergent AMPAR Subunit Adaptation in the NAc to Cocaine and Chronic Stress
proposals.motivation: Chronic cocaine exposure and chronic stress both upregulate GluA2-lacking AMPA receptors in the nucleus accumbens (NAc), a shared molecular adaptation linked to maladaptive behavioral output. The field assumes this common endpoint reflects a common mechanistic pathway, limiting the search for targeted interventions. This project challenges that assumption, positing that distinct afferent circuits and intracellular signaling cascades drive this convergence, which is critical for developing precise, etiology-specific therapeutics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that cocaine-induced and stress-induced upregulation of GluA1 in D1-type medium spiny neurons (MSNs) of the NAc is mediated by distinct patterns of afferent input (e.g., prefrontal cortex vs. amygdala) and engages divergent ΔFosB/CREB-dependent transcriptional programs, despite the shared synaptic endpoint.
proposals.method: We will combine circuit-specific, activity-dependent TRAP2/RiboTag profiling in D1-Cre mice with viral tracing and chemogenetic inhibition to map afferent-specific translatomes following cocaine or chronic social defeat stress. Causal network modeling (using Bayesian network inference algorithms like bnlearn) will integrate this multimodal data—anatomical connectivity, neuronal activity (Fos), and molecular profiles—to reconstruct the upstream signaling pathways (e.g., mTOR, ERK) that are uniquely engaged by each stimulus to regulate GluA1 expression.
proposals.contribution: This work will move the field beyond cataloging shared molecular endpoints to defining the circuit-specific 'logic' that produces them. It will provide a causal map of divergent pathological routes to synaptic maladaptation, identifying novel, input-specific targets (e.g., specific afferent projections or their engaged intracellular kinases) for intervention in addiction versus depression.
proposals.novelty: While prior work from our lab and others has shown that ΔFosB in D1-MSNs integrates diverse stimuli, the proposal to systematically dissect the entirely distinct upstream circuit and signaling architectures that converge on a specific AMPAR subunit is a novel 'circuitomics' approach, shifting the therapeutic focus from the shared endpoint to the divergent routes.

proposals.title: Circuit-Dissociable Inputs to the VTA Drive a Convergent AMPA/NMDA Ratio Increase Following Cocaine and Chronic Stress
proposals.motivation: Both drugs of abuse and chronic stress induce long-lasting adaptations in the mesolimbic dopamine system, including increased AMPA/NMDA receptor ratios in VTA dopamine neurons, a key synaptic correlate of behavioral sensitization. However, it is unknown whether these similar synaptic endpoints arise from adaptations in distinct upstream circuits, which would represent a fundamental principle of neural plasticity where different etiologies converge on a common cellular phenotype via separable pathways.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that cocaine and chronic stress induce a common increase in the AMPA/NMDA ratio of VTA dopamine neurons via distinct afferent pathways: cocaine via glutamatergic inputs from the pedunculopontine tegmental nucleus (PPTg), and stress via inhibitory inputs from the lateral habenula (LHb).
proposals.method: We will use a combination of viral-mediated, cell-type-specific optogenetics and ex vivo patch-clamp electrophysiology in mouse brain slices. Mice will undergo either chronic cocaine administration or a chronic social defeat stress paradigm. In separate cohorts, we will express channelrhodopsin in either LHb GABAergic or PPTg glutamatergic neurons projecting to the VTA. In acute VTA slices, we will perform whole-cell recordings from identified dopamine neurons and optically stimulate these specific pathways to measure evoked AMPA/NMDA ratios. To test causal necessity, we will chemogenetically (using DREADDs) inhibit each pathway during the respective behavioral manipulation and assess if it blocks the synaptic adaptation.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel circuit-level dissociation for how distinct environmental stimuli produce a shared synaptic change, moving beyond correlative observations to causal, input-specific mechanisms. It will directly link specific, behaviorally relevant neural circuits to a core cellular adaptation, providing a more nuanced framework for understanding the pathophysiology of addiction and depression.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has shown that both cocaine and stress increase VTA AMPA/NMDA ratios, no study has causally tested whether these changes originate from separable afferent circuits. This proposal uniquely combines pathway-specific manipulation with precise synaptic physiology to dissect the circuit origins of a convergent synaptic phenotype.

proposals.title: Circuit-Specific Induction of ΔFosB in Nucleus Accumbens Subpopulations by Cocaine versus Chronic Social Defeat Stress
proposals.motivation: Both drugs of abuse and chronic stress produce similar enduring adaptations in the nucleus accumbens (NAc), including the stable accumulation of ΔFosB in D1- and D2-type medium spiny neurons (MSNs), which is thought to drive maladaptive behavioral states. However, it is unknown whether these common molecular endpoints are initiated by convergent or distinct afferent circuit activity. Resolving this is critical for understanding whether therapeutic strategies should target the final common molecular pathway or the distinct upstream neural circuits that drive pathology.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that cocaine exposure and chronic social defeat stress induce ΔFosB in overlapping populations of NAc MSNs via the activation of entirely distinct upstream glutamatergic afferent pathways from the prefrontal cortex, amygdala, and hippocampus.
proposals.method: We will use a combination of circuit-specific chemogenetic inhibition, cell-type-specific translating ribosome affinity purification (TRAP), and neuroproteomics. First, in FosTRAP mice, we will chemogenetically inhibit specific cortical or limbic inputs to the NAc during either cocaine conditioning or social defeat stress to determine which circuits are necessary for ΔFosB induction. Second, we will perform cell-type-specific TRAP-seq on D1- and D2-MSNs from these manipulated animals to profile the immediate early gene and synaptic protein response. Finally, we will use viral-mediated, Cre-dependent ΔFosB overexpression or knockdown in specific MSN subtypes to test the sufficiency of the pathway-specific molecular signatures for driving stress- versus cocaine-related behavioral phenotypes.
proposals.contribution: This work will delineate whether shared molecular adaptations in addiction and depression models arise from convergent or divergent neural circuit activity. It will identify circuit-specific molecular signatures upstream of ΔFosB, providing novel targets for intervention. The findings will refine the 'final common pathway' hypothesis by demonstrating the separable origins of a shared neuroadaptation.
proposals.novelty: While prior work maps where ΔFosB is expressed, this proposal directly tests how it is induced by different stimuli at the circuit level, linking distinct afferent activity to a common transcriptional endpoint. The integration of circuit manipulation with cell-type-specific molecular profiling in the same animal is a novel approach to this question.

proposals.title: ΔFosB-Dependent Synaptic Scaling in NAc D1-MSNs: Distinguishing Compensatory Homeostasis from Pathogenic Sensitization via Longitudinal Functional Network Analysis
proposals.motivation: In addiction and depression research, persistent upregulation of molecules like AMPA receptors and ΔFosB in nucleus accumbens (NAc) D1-MSNs is often interpreted as a pathogenic driver of neural sensitization and maladaptive behavior. However, this view conflates correlation with causation. Pre-2026 work in other systems, like chronic pain, suggests similar molecular changes could instead reflect homeostatic synaptic scaling attempting to restore baseline circuit function. Resolving this ambiguity is critical for developing targeted therapies that disrupt pathogenic processes without impairing compensatory resilience.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that ΔFosB induction in NAc D1-MSNs following chronic stress or cocaine exposure initially drives a compensatory upregulation of synaptic scaffolding proteins and glutamate receptors, which is associated with a normalization of mesolimbic circuit excitability and functional connectivity; a failure of this homeostatic response leads to pathogenic sensitization and behavioral deficits.
proposals.method: We will use a longitudinal design in mice expressing Cre in D1-MSNs. Chronic social defeat stress or cocaine regimen will be applied, with cohorts analyzed at multiple timepoints. Cell-type-specific neuroproteomics (via FACS and mass spectrometry) will quantify synaptic protein changes. In vivo fiber photometry will record calcium dynamics in NAc D1-MSNs, while whole-brain functional connectivity will be assessed via fMRI in awake mice. Causal manipulation of ΔFosB (using viral overexpression or CRISPR-mediated knockdown) at specific timepoints will test its necessity and sufficiency for observed molecular and network signatures. Data integration will use dynamic Bayesian network modeling to infer causal relationships between ΔFosB, synaptic proteins, local excitability, and global connectivity.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel framework for interpreting persistent transcriptional and synaptic adaptations, distinguishing those that are compensatory from those that are pathogenic. It will identify specific functional network signatures predictive of behavioral resilience versus susceptibility. The findings will refine molecular targets for neuropsychiatric disorders, prioritizing interventions that specifically disrupt maladaptive sensitization while preserving adaptive homeostasis.
proposals.novelty: The proposal moves beyond static correlations to define dynamic, causal trajectories of adaptation. It uniquely integrates cell-type-specific molecular profiling with in vivo circuit-level functional readouts and computational modeling to test a homeostatic role for ΔFosB, a factor typically cast solely as a pro-sensitization molecule.

proposals.title: Computational Modeling of Glutamatergic Homeostasis in Locus Coeruleus Withdrawal: Distinguishing Adaptive from Maladaptive Receptor Upregulation
proposals.motivation: Withdrawal from drugs of abuse or hormonal states induces a biphasic dysregulation of locus coeruleus (LC) noradrenergic activity, driving negative affect and relapse. Pre-2026 work shows compensatory upregulation of synaptic proteins, like glutamate receptors, during withdrawal, but it is unknown if this molecular adaptation helps restore homeostasis or instead stabilizes a pathological circuit state. A formal, testable model is needed to resolve this critical ambiguity in addiction and affective disorder neurobiology.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that simulated homeostatic upregulation of AMPA/NMDA receptor density within a dynamical systems model of LC withdrawal will not restore the pre-withdrawal firing baseline but will instead lock the LC network into a distinct, stable pathological attractor state characterized by sensitized reactivity to stress.
proposals.method: We will develop a conductance-based, mean-field model of LC neuronal population dynamics, incorporating known noradrenergic autoregulation and glutamatergic drive. The model will include a dynamic variable for synaptic glutamate receptor density, governed by a homeostatic rule sensitive to firing rate deviation. Using bifurcation analysis and numerical simulation (via XPPAUT or custom Python routines), we will map system attractors under baseline, acute withdrawal, and chronic withdrawal conditions with and without receptor upregulation. Model parameters will be constrained by pre-2026 in vivo electrophysiology and proteomic data from LC tissue.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel computational framework to formally evaluate competing interpretations of molecular plasticity observed in addiction models. It will generate testable predictions about the circuit-level consequences of receptor trafficking for LC network stability and identify potential targets for interventions aimed at steering plasticity toward adaptive, rather than maladaptive, outcomes.
proposals.novelty: While Dr. Nestler's lab has extensively characterized molecular adaptations like ΔFosB in addiction, this proposal uniquely applies dynamical systems theory to formalize how such specific synaptic changes govern the transition between global brain states, moving beyond correlation to mechanistic causality.

proposals.title: Cell-Type-Specific Synaptic Proteomics Reveals Compensatory Glutamate Receptor Upregulation in D1-MSNs Following Chronic Cocaine Exposure
proposals.motivation: Chronic cocaine exposure induces persistent molecular adaptations in the nucleus accumbens (NAc), including increased expression of ΔFosB and glutamate receptors (e.g., GluA2), which are widely interpreted as pro-sensitizing changes driving addiction vulnerability. However, the functional consequence of these receptor changes within specific neural circuits remains ambiguous. This proposal challenges the dominant sensitization hypothesis by testing whether these increases represent a homeostatic counter-adaptation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that cocaine-induced upregulation of synaptic AMPA receptors in D1-type medium spiny neurons (D1-MSNs) of the NAc is a compensatory, homeostatic response that opposes behavioral and synaptic sensitization, rather than a primary driver of it.
proposals.method: We will combine cell-type-specific translating ribosome affinity purification (TRAP) and quantitative neuroproteomics to profile synaptic proteins from NAc D1-MSNs of mice following chronic cocaine. We will correlate proteomic changes with in vivo electrophysiology to assess synaptic scaling. Using Cre-dependent viral vectors in Fosb-tTA transgenic mice, we will selectively overexpress or knockdown ΔFosB in D1-MSNs and measure consequent changes in glutamate receptor subunits and excitatory synaptic drive via patch-clamp electrophysiology. Finally, we will use chemogenetics (DREADDs) to acutely modulate D1-MSN activity during cocaine withdrawal and assess its impact on both receptor expression and behavioral sensitization.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, circuit-specific reinterpretation of a canonical addiction-related molecular adaptation. It will directly test the causal role of ΔFosB in regulating synaptic homeostasis versus sensitization. The findings could shift the therapeutic focus from broadly suppressing plasticity to stabilizing specific homeostatic mechanisms in defined cell populations.
proposals.novelty: While prior work links ΔFosB and GluA2 increases to sensitization, the idea that this is a compensatory, anti-sensitization mechanism is counter-intuitive and directly challenges the prevailing model. The approach is novel in integrating cell-type-specific proteomics, precise ΔFosB manipulation, and functional readouts to dissect cause from consequence.

proposals.title: Cell-Type-Specific Spatiotemporal Dynamics of AMPAR Trafficking in NAc D1-MSNs Link Cocaine-Induced Synaptic Remodeling to Altered Functional Connectivity and Behavior
proposals.motivation: Chronic cocaine exposure induces persistent adaptations in the nucleus accumbens (NAc), including altered glutamatergic transmission and stable transcription factor expression like ΔFosB in D1-type medium spiny neurons (D1-MSNs), which are critical for reward and motivation. However, the real-time, subcellular dynamics of glutamate receptor trafficking that underlie these synaptic changes and their direct causal relationship to circuit-wide functional connectivity and specific behavioral outputs remain unknown. Bridging this gap is essential for understanding the precise synaptic mechanisms of addiction pathology.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that chronic cocaine exposure drives cell-type-specific, spatiotemporally defined patterns of AMPA receptor (AMPAR) insertion and removal at individual synapses on NAc D1-MSNs, and that these trafficking events directly determine both the strength of specific synaptic inputs and the emergence of altered whole-brain functional connectivity and cocaine-seeking behavior.
proposals.method: We will use Cre-dependent viral vectors to express fluorescently tagged, pH-sensitive GluA2 subunits (pHluorin-GluA2) specifically in D1-MSNs of the NAc core in Fosb-Cre mice. In vivo two-photon imaging through a chronic cranial window will track receptor trafficking at individual spines in awake mice during and after chronic cocaine administration. Longitudinal imaging data will be temporally aligned with in vivo electrophysiology (local field potentials) from the NAc and prefrontal cortex and with behavioral performance on a probabilistic reward task. Functional connectivity changes will be assessed using fMRI, as in prior Nestler lab work, correlated with the synaptic trafficking timeline.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first direct, causal link between the real-time dynamics of receptor trafficking in a defined cell type, the resulting synaptic plasticity, and the reorganization of brain-wide networks in a disease model. It will identify specific spatiotemporal patterns of receptor movement as critical drivers of the maladaptive synaptic and behavioral plasticity induced by cocaine.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has measured bulk receptor levels or static synaptic changes, this proposal integrates real-time, subcellular visualization of receptor trafficking in a behaviorally relevant cell type with longitudinal circuit and behavioral readouts, moving beyond correlation to test direct causality in vivo.

proposals.title: Quantitative modeling of cocaine-induced glutamate receptor dynamics in the NAc using ecological succession frameworks
proposals.motivation: Chronic cocaine exposure induces persistent neuroadaptations in the nucleus accumbens (NAc), including upregulation of AMPA and NMDA receptor subunits, which are critical for enduring behavioral plasticity. However, the precise temporal trajectories of these changes and the point at which they become stable remain poorly defined, limiting our ability to target critical windows for intervention. This gap mirrors classic problems in ecology, where predicting the recovery or transition of an ecosystem after a disturbance requires rigorous temporal mapping and modeling.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the time-course of GluR1 and NMDAR1 upregulation in D1-type medium spiny neurons (MSNs) of the NAc following repeated cocaine exposure follows non-linear, saturating growth curves analogous to ecological succession, and that the induction of ΔFosB marks a transition point to a stable, maladaptive synaptic state.
proposals.method: We will use a longitudinal design in Fosb-CreERT2 mice, administering cocaine or saline and collecting NAc tissue at multiple time points (e.g., 1, 3, 7, 14 days). Cell-type-specific neuroproteomics (via TRAP or FACS) on D1-MSNs will quantify GluR1 and NMDAR1. ΔFosB induction will be monitored in parallel. Time-series expression data will be fitted to ecological models (e.g., logistic growth, Lotka-Volterra competition) using non-linear regression in R to identify model parameters (carrying capacity, growth rate) and inflection points. Functional connectivity changes from a parallel fMRI study will be correlated with model-predicted transition points.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative, predictive framework for the progression of cocaine-induced synaptic alterations, defining critical temporal windows for plasticity. It will directly link the molecular timeline of receptor upregulation with the transcription factor ΔFosB and whole-brain functional outcomes. The application of ecological models offers a novel toolkit for conceptualizing and intervening in neuropsychiatric disease progression.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit cross-disciplinary application of well-established ecological succession and population growth models to synaptic neurobiology, moving beyond descriptive correlation to predictive, quantitative modeling of receptor dynamics in a defined neural population.

proposals.title: Temporal Coupling of Cell-Type-Specific Glutamate Receptor Trafficking and Synaptic Plasticity in the VTA During Cocaine Sensitization
proposals.motivation: Repeated cocaine exposure induces persistent synaptic plasticity in the ventral tegmental area (VTA), a critical substrate for behavioral sensitization. While changes in AMPA/NMDA receptor ratios are documented, the precise molecular timeline of receptor subunit trafficking (e.g., GluR1, NMDAR1) in specific VTA cell types (dopamine vs. GABA neurons) and its causal relationship to functional plasticity remain poorly defined. Bridging this gap is essential for understanding the sequence of neuroadaptations underlying addiction vulnerability.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that repeated cocaine administration drives a temporally precise, cell-type-specific sequence of glutamate receptor subunit synaptic insertion in the VTA, and that these molecular events are the primary drivers of the observed changes in synaptic strength and AMPA/NMDA ratios.
proposals.method: We will use a cocaine sensitization protocol in TH-Cre and VGAT-Cre mice. At defined time points (e.g., after 1, 5, 10 injections and 1-14 days withdrawal), VTA tissue will be processed for subcellular fractionation to isolate synaptic membranes, followed by quantitative immunoblotting for GluR1 and NMDAR1, stratified by cell type via fluorescence-activated sorting of tagged nuclei (INTACT method). In parallel cohorts, VTA slices prepared at identical time points will be used for targeted patch-clamp recordings from identified dopamine and GABA neurons to measure AMPA/NMDA ratios, paired-pulse ratio, and miniature EPSCs. Correlative analysis will link molecular trafficking data directly to electrophysiological outcomes.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first high-resolution, cell-type-specific map linking the dynamics of glutamate receptor trafficking to functional synaptic plasticity in the VTA during drug exposure. It will identify critical molecular windows that gate persistent neuroadaptations. The findings will directly inform the neuroproteomic mechanisms underlying ΔFosB- and CREB-mediated gene regulation in addiction models.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct temporal and cell-type-specific correlation between in vivo receptor trafficking and ex vivo electrophysiology within the same experimental timeline, moving beyond descriptive parallel observations to test a mechanistic sequence. This fusion approach is not standard in the field.

proposals.title: Circuit-Level Redundancy in Stress-Amphetamine Cross-Sensitization: A Distributed VTA Network Perspective
proposals.motivation: Cross-sensitization between stress and psychostimulants is a core process in addiction and comorbid mood disorders, but its neural basis remains poorly resolved. Pre-2026 work, including our own on ΔFosB in nucleus accumbens (NAc) neurons, shows that both stress and drugs induce persistent molecular adaptations, yet attempts to block sensitization by targeting single pathways have often failed. This suggests a resilient, distributed circuit mechanism that current models do not capture.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that stress-amphetamine cross-sensitization is maintained by redundant synaptic plasticity distributed across parallel VTA output circuits, such that inhibition of one pathway triggers compensatory plasticity in others, preserving the sensitized behavioral state.
proposals.method: We will use a sequential stress-amphetamine exposure protocol in FosTRAP2::Ai14 mice to label activated ensembles. Circuit-specific chemogenetic inhibition (DREADDs) will be applied to defined VTA→NAc (D1/D2 MSN), VTA→mPFC, and VTA→amygdala pathways during sensitization. In parallel, we will use a CRISPR interference approach (dCas9-KRAB) to disrupt glutamate receptor (e.g., GluA1) trafficking postsynaptically in these target neurons. In vivo fiber photometry of calcium dynamics (GCaMP) across this VTA-centered network will be performed throughout, correlating circuit activity with locomotor sensitization. A sequential inhibition design will test for compensatory recruitment.
proposals.contribution: This work will move the field beyond single-locus models by mapping a functional redundancy network for maladaptive plasticity. It will identify which circuit nodes are necessary, sufficient, or compensatory for cross-sensitization, providing a more complete circuit diagram for intervention. The integration of chemogenetic, CRISPR-based, and in vivo monitoring approaches will establish a new methodological framework for probing circuit resilience.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has mapped molecular changes (e.g., ΔFosB, CREB) in specific cell types, the explicit test for distributed, compensatory plasticity at the circuit level is novel. The combined use of activity-dependent labeling, pathway-specific CRISPR interference on synaptic proteins, and network-level calcium dynamics to dissect redundancy represents a significant conceptual and technical advance over existing studies.

proposals.title: VTA Glutamate Receptor Upregulation as a Convergent Mechanism for Stress-Drug Cross-Sensitization: A ΔFosB-Dependent Investigation
proposals.motivation: Cross-sensitization between stress and drugs of abuse, such as cocaine, is a core process driving addiction vulnerability and relapse, but its precise neural mechanisms remain unclear. Pre-2026 work from our lab and others implicates ΔFosB in D1-type medium spiny neurons (MSNs) of the nucleus accumbens (NAc) as a critical, persistent transcription factor in this process. However, upstream signaling events in the ventral tegmental area (VTA), a primary locus for synaptic plasticity induced by both stress and drugs, are poorly defined, particularly regarding glutamate receptor dynamics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that stress- and drug-induced cross-sensitization requires a convergent upregulation of AMPA and/or NMDA receptor subunits in VTA dopamine neurons, a process mediated by the induction of ΔFosB in downstream NAc D1-MSNs that subsequently modulates VTA afferent activity via striato-midbrain pathways.
proposals.method: We will use a established mouse model of cross-sensitization, combining chronic social defeat stress with subsequent cocaine administration. Using Cre-dependent viral vectors in Fosb-floxed and D1-Cre mice, we will first manipulate ΔFosB specifically in NAc D1-MSNs and assess behavioral sensitization and VTA glutamate receptor subunit (GluA1, GluN1) expression via immunohistochemistry and synaptic neuroproteomics. Second, we will use intra-VTA microinfusion of receptor antagonists (e.g., NBQX, D-AP5) or AAV-shRNA targeting specific subunits during the sensitization protocol to test causal necessity. Whole-brain functional connectivity, as in our recent work, will be assessed using resting-state fMRI to determine how blocking VTA glutamate upregulation alters the mesocorticolimbic networks shaped by ΔFosB.
proposals.contribution: This study will directly link a specific molecular adaptation in the VTA (glutamate receptor upregulation) to the enduring transcriptional signature (ΔFosB) in the NAc that underlies cross-sensitization. It will provide a mechanistic circuit model from VTA plasticity to NAc transcription to whole-brain network change, offering novel targets for intervention in stress-precipitated addiction.
proposals.novelty: While ΔFosB in the NAc is known to be important, the proposal uniquely positions VTA glutamate receptor upregulation as a necessary, convergent upstream trigger for cross-sensitization that is functionally gated by striatal ΔFosB, integrating molecular, cellular, and systems-level analysis.

proposals.title: Cell-type and projection-specific mapping of glutamate receptor plasticity in VTA neurons following cocaine exposure
proposals.motivation: The ventral tegmental area (VTA) is a heterogeneous hub for reward and aversion, yet our understanding of drug-induced synaptic plasticity remains constrained by the dopaminergic/GABAergic binary. Pre-2026 work, including from our lab on ΔFosB in the NAc, shows that molecular adaptations are highly cell-type-specific. A critical gap is whether key glutamate receptor subunits (GluR1, NMDAR1) undergo subtype-specific changes in VTA neurons defined by both molecular identity and long-range projections, which could explain circuit-specific contributions to addiction.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that cocaine exposure induces distinct patterns of glutamate receptor subunit expression, synaptic localization, and functional plasticity in VTA neuron subpopulations, and that these changes are segregated not by broad neurotransmitter class but by specific molecular profiles and projection targets.
proposals.method: We will use intersectional viral strategies in transgenic mice (e.g., DAT-Cre x RC::FPDi) to label and manipulate molecularly defined VTA subpopulations (e.g., TH+, VGluT2+). Retrograde tracing from target regions (NAc, mPFC, amygdala) will further stratify neurons by projection. We will perform single-nucleus RNA sequencing (snRNA-seq) on FACS-sorted, projection-defined populations to create a high-resolution molecular atlas. In parallel, we will use high-resolution STORM imaging and slice electrophysiology (measuring AMPA/NMDA ratios and mEPSCs) in these precisely identified cells to quantify receptor dynamics after chronic cocaine.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first integrated map linking VTA neuron molecular identity, projection anatomy, and cocaine-induced glutamate receptor plasticity. It will identify specific neural ensembles within the VTA that are primary sites of synaptic adaptation, moving the field beyond monolithic models. This could reveal novel, circuit-specific targets for intervention in substance use disorders.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the simultaneous integration of three defining features—molecular subtype, projection target, and subunit-specific receptor plasticity—within the same VTA neurons, a resolution not achieved in pre-2026 studies which typically examined these variables in isolation.

proposals.title: Cell-Type-Specific Proteomic Mapping of Glutamate Receptor Complexes in VTA Dopaminergic and GABAergic Neurons Following Cocaine Exposure
proposals.motivation: Chronic cocaine exposure induces long-lasting adaptations in the ventral tegmental area (VTA), including changes in glutamatergic signaling and transcription factors like ΔFosB and CREB. However, existing methods (e.g., electrophysiology, bulk RNA-seq) cannot determine if receptor subunit composition and associated protein complexes change in a cell-type-specific manner, which is critical for understanding circuit-specific plasticity underlying addiction.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that chronic cocaine administration will induce distinct, cell-type-specific changes in the protein interactomes of AMPA (GluR1) and NMDA (NMDAR1) receptor subunits in VTA dopaminergic versus GABAergic neurons, providing a molecular substrate for drug-induced synaptic plasticity.
proposals.method: We will generate Cre-dependent AAV vectors encoding TurboID fused to GluR1 or NMDAR1. These will be stereotaxically injected into the VTA of DAT-Cre or VGAT-Cre mice to restrict expression to dopaminergic or GABAergic neurons, respectively. Following chronic cocaine or saline administration, in vivo biotinylation will be performed, VTA will be microdissected, and biotinylated proteins will be purified via streptavidin beads. Quantitative mass spectrometry (TMT labeling) will identify and compare receptor-proximal proteomes from each cell type and condition.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, proteomic census of cell-type-specific glutamate receptor complexes in the VTA, linking transcriptional regulators like ΔFosB to specific synaptic protein networks. It will move the field beyond indirect measures of receptor changes, offering a mechanistic blueprint for how distinct VTA cell populations remodel their synaptic inputs in response to drugs of abuse.
proposals.novelty: While proximity labeling is used for subcellular proteomics, its application to map cell-type-specific receptor interactomes in vivo in defined neuronal populations of the VTA is novel. This directly bridges the gap between Dr. Nestler's expertise in transcriptional regulation (ΔFosB/CREB) and the need to understand resulting synaptic protein changes.

proposals.title: Cell-Type-Specific Quantification of Synaptic Surface AMPARs in D1-MSNs Following Chronic Cocaine: A Subcellular Fractionation-Enhanced Biotinylation Approach
proposals.motivation: Chronic cocaine exposure induces persistent adaptations in the nucleus accumbens (NAc), including alterations in AMPA receptor (AMPAR) subunit composition and trafficking, which are critical for enduring behavioral plasticity. However, existing surface biotinylation methods applied to whole-tissue homogenates cannot distinguish between receptors at the synaptic membrane and those in intracellular pools within the nerve terminal, limiting our understanding of the precise synaptic changes driven by transcription factors like ΔFosB in specific neuronal populations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that combining cell-type-specific isolation of synaptosomes from D1-medium spiny neurons (D1-MSNs) with subsequent surface biotinylation will reveal a specific increase in synaptic surface GluA2-lacking AMPARs in the NAc following chronic cocaine, a change that is more pronounced and temporally distinct than measurements from whole-tissue biotinylation.
proposals.method: We will use D1-Cre mice crossed to a synaptosome-tagging line (e.g., RiboTag) to immunopurify D1-MSN synaptosomes from NAc homogenates following sucrose gradient centrifugation. This synaptosomal fraction will then undergo surface biotinylation with a membrane-impermeable reagent (e.g., Sulfo-NHS-SS-Biotin). Biotinylated surface proteins will be captured on streptavidin beads and quantified via western blot for AMPAR subunits (GluA1, GluA2). This will be compared to standard whole-tissue biotinylation and total synaptosomal protein levels. Experiments will be conducted in saline- and cocaine-treated mice, with ΔFosB manipulation (viral overexpression/knockdown) in D1-MSNs.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, high-resolution method for quantifying cell-type-specific synaptic surface proteomes, directly applicable to neuroproteomics. It will yield a more precise map of cocaine-induced AMPAR redistribution specifically at D1-MSN synapses, linking ΔFosB-mediated transcription to functional synaptic changes. This could identify new, synapse-specific targets for intervention in addiction.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the sequential, two-step purification: first isolating intact synaptic terminals from a defined cell type, then applying surface labeling specifically to this fraction. This bypasses the contamination from non-synaptic membranes and intracellular pools from other cell types that confounds traditional whole-tissue biotinylation.

proposals.title: Chemogenetic Dissection of GluR1+ VTA Neuron Activity in the Expression of Amphetamine Behavioral Sensitization
proposals.motivation: Behavioral sensitization to psychostimulants is a core model for the persistent neural adaptations underlying addiction vulnerability and relapse. While ΔFosB accumulation in nucleus accumbens (NAc) D1-type medium spiny neurons (MSNs) is a known molecular substrate, the upstream circuits driving the *expression* of the sensitized state remain poorly defined. Pre-2026 work implicates VTA glutamate signaling, but the necessity of activity in specific VTA neuronal subpopulations at the precise moment of expression has not been causally tested, limiting circuit-level understanding.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that acute activity in VTA neurons expressing the AMPA receptor subunit GluR1 is necessary for the behavioral expression of established amphetamine sensitization, acting as a critical driver of downstream NAc ΔFosB-related circuitry.
proposals.method: We will use GluR1-Cre transgenic mice to achieve cell-type-specific targeting. A Cre-dependent AAV encoding the inhibitory DREADD hM4Di-mCherry will be stereotaxically injected into the VTA. After recovery, mice will undergo a standard amphetamine sensitization protocol (5 daily injections). Following a 7-10 day withdrawal, during a final amphetamine challenge to test for sensitized locomotor expression, mice will receive CNO to acutely inhibit GluR1+ VTA neurons. Control groups will receive saline or lack DREADD expression. Locomotor activity will be quantified, and brains will be processed for FosB/ΔFosB immunohistochemistry in NAc D1 and D2 MSNs to link circuit manipulation to molecular endpoints.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first causal evidence for the necessity of a defined VTA neuronal population in the expression, not just induction, of behavioral sensitization. It will directly link acute activity in a specific glutamate-receptor-defined circuit node to the persistent molecular adaptations (e.g., ΔFosB) in the NAc that are central to Nestler's research themes, moving beyond correlation to establish a temporally precise circuit mechanism.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the temporally precise, chemogenetic intervention targeting a molecularly defined VTA subpopulation *specifically during the expression phase* of sensitization, overcoming the interpretational confounds of permanent lesions or chronic manipulations used in prior studies.

proposals.title: Circuit-Specific Causal Dissection of VTA GluR1 in Cocaine Sensitization Using Barcoded Viral Tracing and Single-Cell Transcriptomics
proposals.motivation: Behavioral sensitization to cocaine is a core model for addiction-related plasticity, with the ventral tegmental area (VTA) and its projections being critical. However, pre-2026 studies, including our work on ΔFosB in nucleus accumbens (NAc) neurons, highlight that circuit-specific adaptations, not just regional changes, drive behavior. Current methods for manipulating proteins like GluR1 lack the resolution to causally link molecular alterations in defined VTA output pathways to sensitization, creating a major knowledge gap.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that GluR1 upregulation in VTA dopamine neurons projecting to the NAc core, but not other targets, is necessary and sufficient for the induction of locomotor sensitization to cocaine.
proposals.method: We will generate a library of AAVs expressing Cre-dependent, barcoded constructs for either GluR1 overexpression or shRNA-mediated knockdown, alongside a fluorescent reporter. Using a dual-vector intersectional strategy, we will inject a retrograde Cre virus into the NAc core (or other targets) and the barcoded library into the VTA of DAT-Cre mice, restricting manipulation to specific projections. Following a cocaine sensitization protocol, we will perform single-nucleus RNA sequencing on VTA tissue to simultaneously read the viral barcode (identifying the manipulated circuit and construct) and the transcriptional state of infected neurons. Behavioral outcomes will be correlated with molecular and circuit-specific perturbations.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a causal, projection-specific role for GluR1 in addiction-related plasticity, moving beyond correlative regional studies. It will provide a novel methodological framework—integrating viral barcoding, circuit targeting, and single-cell genomics—for high-resolution causal testing of molecular hypotheses in defined neural pathways, directly extending our lab's focus on cell-type-specific adaptations (e.g., in D1/D2 MSNs) to the presynaptic circuitry.
proposals.novelty: While viral barcoding is used for lineage tracing, its adaptation for causal, circuit-specific molecular manipulation and high-throughput readout via single-cell sequencing is novel. This approach transcends traditional lesion or regional pharmacology by enabling simultaneous testing of necessity and sufficiency across multiple parallel circuits within a single animal.

proposals.title: Cell-Type-Specific Synaptic Adaptations in VTA Dopamine Neurons Following ΔFosB Induction by Cocaine and Stress
proposals.motivation: The transcription factor ΔFosB is a critical, persistent molecular switch induced in the nucleus accumbens (NAc) by chronic cocaine and stress, driving long-term neural and behavioral adaptations. However, its downstream effects on synaptic function in upstream brain regions that project to the NAc, particularly the ventral tegmental area (VTA), remain poorly defined. Understanding how ΔFosB induction in NAc D1-type medium spiny neurons (MSNs) alters synaptic plasticity in VTA dopamine neurons is essential for a circuit-level model of addiction and depression comorbidity.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that chronic cocaine exposure and chronic stress, both of which induce ΔFosB in NAc D1-MSNs, will produce cell-type-specific and input-specific alterations in AMPA/NMDA receptor ratios at excitatory synapses onto VTA dopamine neurons, and that these electrophysiological changes will be dependent on ΔFosB expression in the NAc.
proposals.method: We will use ex vivo whole-cell patch-clamp recordings in VTA slices from transgenic mice (e.g., TH-Cre for dopamine neuron identification). Mice will undergo repeated cocaine or chronic social defeat stress protocols. To test ΔFosB dependence, we will use viral-Cre strategies in Fosb-lox mice to selectively knock down ΔFosB in NAc D1-MSNs. We will measure AMPA/NMDA ratios by recording evoked excitatory postsynaptic currents (EPSCs) at -70mV and +40mV, isolating components with NBQX and APV. Input specificity will be assessed using optogenetic stimulation of defined afferents (e.g., from lateral hypothalamus or pedunculopontine tegmental nucleus) in channelrhodopsin-expressing mice.
proposals.contribution: This work will directly link a key addiction-related transcription factor in the NAc to functional synaptic remodeling in the VTA, providing a mechanistic circuit explanation for how persistent molecular changes in one node alter information processing in another. It will reveal whether convergent molecular adaptations (ΔFosB) from distinct etiologies (drugs, stress) produce similar or divergent synaptic phenotypes, informing models of shared neuropathology.
proposals.novelty: While ΔFosB's role in the NAc is well-studied, its trans-synaptic impact on VTA physiology is unknown. Combining cell-type-specific ΔFosB manipulation, defined circuit optogenetics, and detailed synaptic physiology in the VTA offers a novel, circuit-mechanistic perspective beyond correlative studies in single brain regions.

proposals.title: Cell-Type-Specific Mapping of Neuroadaptation: Multiplexed FISH for VTA Subunit mRNA Localization in Cocaine-Exposed Mice
proposals.motivation: Chronic cocaine exposure induces long-lasting transcriptional adaptations in the ventral tegmental area (VTA), a heterogeneous region containing dopaminergic, GABAergic, and glutamatergic neurons. Standard in situ hybridization lacks the resolution to assign these molecular changes to specific neuronal populations, creating a critical gap in understanding the precise cellular substrates of addiction-related plasticity. Resolving this is essential for linking specific gene regulation, such as ΔFosB or CREB target genes, to defined circuits driving behavioral states.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that cocaine-induced changes in the expression of key synaptic and transcription factor subunit mRNAs (e.g., Fosb, Creb isoforms, glutamate receptor subunits) are not uniformly distributed across the VTA but are instead localized to specific phenotypically defined neuronal subpopulations, particularly D1-projecting dopaminergic neurons.
proposals.method: We will employ high-sensitivity, multiplexed fluorescent in situ hybridization (RNAscope) to visualize up to four target mRNAs simultaneously in VTA sections from saline- and cocaine-exposed mice. This will be integrated with immunofluorescence for definitive phenotypic markers: tyrosine hydroxylase (TH) for dopaminergic neurons, vGluT2 for glutamatergic neurons, and GAD67 for GABAergic neurons. Cell segmentation and co-localization analysis using established image analysis pipelines (e.g., in CellProfiler or FIJI) will quantify mRNA signal intensity within each defined neuronal class. Validation will involve mice expressing Cre-dependent reporters in specific VTA subpopulations.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, cell-type-resolved map of cocaine-induced transcriptional changes within the VTA's mixed neuronal landscape. It will directly test whether molecular neuroadaptations previously attributed broadly to the VTA are specific to its dopaminergic output neurons or involve other local cell types. This will refine models of addiction neurobiology and identify more precise cellular targets for intervention.
proposals.novelty: While multiplexed FISH is used in developmental biology and spatial transcriptomics, its systematic application to dissect drug-induced molecular plasticity within a defined, phenotypically mixed brain nucleus like the VTA is novel. This approach moves beyond bulk tissue or genetically targeted sequencing by preserving spatial context and enabling direct visual correlation of mRNA changes with protein-defined cell identity.

proposals.title: Single-Cell Transcriptomic Profiling of Glutamate Receptor Subunit Dynamics in VTA Subtypes Following Cocaine Exposure
proposals.motivation: The Ventral Tegmental Area (VTA) is a heterogeneous hub for reward and aversion, but our understanding of its molecular adaptations to drugs of abuse is limited by bulk-tissue analyses that obscure cell-type-specific changes. Glutamate receptor subunit composition critically regulates synaptic plasticity, yet how it varies across VTA dopaminergic (DA), GABAergic, and glutamatergic neurons, and how it is altered by cocaine exposure, remains unknown. This gap prevents a mechanistic link between specific transcriptional programs, like ΔFosB induction, and cell-type-specific synaptic changes underlying addiction.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that distinct VTA neuronal subtypes possess unique baseline transcriptomic signatures for glutamate receptor subunits (e.g., Gria1-4, Grin1-3), and that cocaine exposure induces cell-type-specific, ΔFosB-associated shifts in these signatures, which correlate with functional connectivity changes in downstream targets like the nucleus accumbens.
proposals.method: We will use fluorescence-activated nucleus sorting (FANS) to isolate nuclei from the VTA of transgenic mice (e.g., TH-Cre, VGAT-Cre) and mice exposed to chronic cocaine or saline, followed by droplet-based single-nucleus RNA sequencing (snRNA-seq, 10x Genomics). Bioinformatic analysis will involve clustering (Seurat, Scanpy) to define neuronal subtypes, differential expression analysis (MAST, DESeq2) to identify cocaine-induced changes in receptor subunits and Fosb isoforms, and trajectory inference (Monocle3) to model transcriptional state transitions. Findings will be validated by multiplexed fluorescent in situ hybridization (RNAScope) and linked to functional data via ex vivo electrophysiology.
proposals.contribution: This work will generate the first single-cell resolution map of glutamate receptor expression in the VTA, defining molecular heterogeneity at an unprecedented scale. It will directly link cocaine-induced ΔFosB expression in specific cell types to precise alterations in synaptic receptor machinery, providing a causal molecular framework for understanding how drug experience rewires specific VTA microcircuits. This bridges the gap between transcriptional regulators like ΔFosB/CREB and the synaptic changes they orchestrate.
proposals.novelty: While Dr. Nestler's lab has studied ΔFosB and CREB in bulk VTA and NAc, applying single-cell transcriptomics to profile the complete glutamate receptor landscape in defined VTA subtypes after cocaine is novel. This moves beyond measuring a few known genes to an unsupervised discovery of cell-type-specific molecular adaptations, directly analogous to resolving tumor microenvironment heterogeneity in cancer immunology.

proposals.title: A Cell-Type-Specific, Quantitative Synaptoproteomic Map of Cocaine-Induced Neuroadaptations in the Nucleus Accumbens
proposals.motivation: Repeated drug exposure induces persistent changes in synaptic function within the nucleus accumbens (NAc), a core hub of the reward circuit, driving addictive behaviors. While transcription factors like ΔFosB and CREB are known mediators, the full spectrum of drug-regulated synaptic proteins—the ultimate effectors of neural plasticity—remains poorly characterized at a proteome-wide scale. A systematic, unbiased identification of these proteins is critical for understanding addiction's molecular underpinnings and identifying novel therapeutic targets.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that repeated cocaine administration drives a specific, cell-type-restricted reorganization of the synaptic proteome in D1- and D2-type medium spiny neurons (MSNs) of the NAc, and that these proteomic signatures are distinct from those induced by other salient stimuli, such as hormonal state changes.
proposals.method: We will employ a cell-type-specific, proximity-based biotinylation approach (e.g., TurboID) in D1-Cre and D2-Cre mice to selectively label synaptic proteins in NAc MSN subtypes. Following repeated cocaine or saline administration, synaptoneurosomes will be prepared, biotinylated proteins purified via streptavidin, and analyzed by tandem mass tag (TMT)-based quantitative mass spectrometry. Bioinformatic analysis will use established algorithms (e.g., Perseus, DAVID) for differential expression, pathway enrichment (KEGG, GO), and protein-protein interaction network mapping (STRING) to identify cocaine-regulated synaptic modules.
proposals.contribution: This study will generate the first comprehensive, cell-type-resolved map of cocaine-induced synaptic proteome alterations, moving beyond candidate studies. It will identify novel protein complexes and pathways involved in addiction-related plasticity and determine the degree of overlap between drug-induced and estradiol withdrawal-induced synaptic proteomic changes, informing mechanistic specificity.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has characterized ΔFosB or CREB regulation in specific cell types, this proposal uniquely integrates cell-type-specific proteomic labeling with whole-synapse quantitative proteomics to systematically discover the effector proteins downstream of these transcription factors in a drug exposure model.

proposals.title: Temporal Dynamics of Glutamate Receptor Regulation in D1 and D2 MSNs During Protracted Cocaine Withdrawal
proposals.motivation: Acute cocaine exposure induces well-characterized upregulation of AMPA and NMDA receptor subunits in the nucleus accumbens (NAc), contributing to synaptic potentiation and behavioral sensitization. However, the long-term synaptic adaptations during protracted withdrawal, a critical period for vulnerability to relapse, remain poorly understood. This gap is significant because enduring molecular changes, potentially including a reversal or overshoot of initial adaptations, may underlie the persistent neurobiological substrate of addiction.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that prolonged cocaine withdrawal will induce a cell-type-specific downregulation of glutamate receptor subunits in NAc medium spiny neurons (MSNs), which will correlate with the persistent expression of ΔFosB and behavioral indices of anhedonia and incubation of craving.
proposals.method: We will use a cocaine self-administration and protracted withdrawal model in Fosb-TRAP mice. Following 4-6 weeks of withdrawal, we will employ cell-type-specific translating ribosome affinity purification (TRAP) from D1- and D2-MSNs, followed by quantitative neuroproteomics to profile synaptic glutamate receptor subunits (e.g., GluA1, GluA2, GluN1, GluN2B). Parallel cohorts will undergo behavioral testing for sucrose preference and cue-induced reinstatement. Data will be integrated using multivariate regression analysis to link receptor expression profiles with ΔFosB levels and behavioral outcomes.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first comprehensive, cell-type-specific map of glutamate receptor proteomic changes during protracted withdrawal, moving beyond acute adaptations. It will establish a direct molecular link between persistent transcription factor activity (ΔFosB) and long-term synaptic remodeling, offering new targets for interventions aimed at the enduring withdrawal state.
proposals.novelty: While prior work focuses on receptor upregulation after acute drug exposure, this proposal systematically investigates the inverse, long-term proteomic trajectory. It uniquely integrates cell-specific proteomics, persistent ΔFosB signaling, and protracted behavioral phenotypes to define the synaptic signature of the withdrawal state.

proposals.title: Cell-Type-Specific Mapping of Drug-Potentiated Glutamatergic Afferents to the Ventral Tegmental Area
proposals.motivation: Chronic drug exposure induces long-lasting synaptic adaptations in the ventral tegmental area (VTA), a key node in the brain's reward circuitry. While postsynaptic changes in glutamate receptor expression and function in VTA dopamine neurons are well-documented, the specific presynaptic sources of glutamate whose inputs are potentiated by drugs of abuse remain poorly defined. Identifying these critical afferent pathways is essential for constructing a complete circuit-level understanding of addiction pathology.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that chronic cocaine administration selectively potentiates glutamatergic inputs to VTA dopamine neurons from specific, anatomically defined brain regions, and that this potentiation is regulated in a cell-type-specific manner by transcription factors such as ΔFosB and CREB within the afferent neurons.
proposals.method: We will employ a monosynaptic rabies virus-based retrograde tracing strategy, specifically targeting VTA dopamine neurons via Cre-dependent expression in TH-Cre mice, to comprehensively map glutamatergic afferents. To identify drug-potentiated inputs, we will combine this with ex vivo whole-cell patch-clamp electrophysiology in VTA slices, using optogenetic stimulation of channelrhodopsin-expressing axons from candidate regions (e.g., lateral hypothalamus, pedunculopontine tegmental nucleus, bed nucleus of the stria terminalis) following chronic cocaine or saline treatment. Synaptic strength will be assayed by measuring AMPA/NMDA receptor ratios and paired-pulse ratios. Finally, we will use viral vectors to manipulate ΔFosB or CREB activity selectively within identified afferent populations to test their necessity for cocaine-induced synaptic strengthening.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first functional and anatomical map of which glutamatergic pathways to the VTA are strengthened by cocaine, moving beyond a focus on postsynaptic VTA neurons. It will establish a causal link between transcription factor dynamics in specific afferent populations and the induction of presynaptic plasticity, offering novel targets for circuit-specific intervention in addiction.
proposals.novelty: While previous work has characterized postsynaptic changes in the VTA, this proposal directly addresses the complementary 'dual problem' by identifying the presynaptic origin of drug-potentiated inputs. It innovatively integrates modern viral tracing, cell-type-specific optogenetics, and transcription factor manipulation to dissect the presynaptic component of drug-induced circuit plasticity.

proposals.title: Epigenetic Scarring by Transient cAMP Signaling in D1-MSNs Mediates Protracted Cocaine Withdrawal
proposals.motivation: Protracted withdrawal from cocaine, characterized by anhedonia and craving, is a major driver of relapse, yet its molecular basis is poorly understood. While acute withdrawal involves transient upregulation of cAMP-CREB signaling in the nucleus accumbens (NAc), this pathway normalizes despite persistent behavioral deficits, suggesting a distinct, stable mechanism maintains the withdrawal state. This proposal tests the novel hypothesis that the initial cAMP surge establishes a lasting 'molecular scar' via epigenetic reprogramming, analogous to developmental fate commitment.
proposals.hypothesis: The transient cAMP-CREB signal during early cocaine withdrawal induces stable, cell-type-specific epigenetic modifications and gene expression programs in D1-type medium spiny neurons (D1-MSNs) of the NAc, and these persistent changes—not sustained cAMP signaling—are necessary and sufficient for protracted withdrawal behaviors.
proposals.method: We will use FosTRAP2 mice crossed with D1-Cre lines to permanently label and isolate NAc D1-MSNs activated during early withdrawal for cell-type-specific neuroproteomics and epigenomic profiling (ATAC-seq, H3K27ac ChIP-seq). Bioinformatic analysis will identify persistently altered regulatory elements and target genes. Causality will be tested by using CRISPR-dCas9 tools (e.g., dCas9-KRAB) to selectively reverse candidate epigenetic marks in D1-MSNs during protracted withdrawal and assess rescue of affective behaviors.
proposals.contribution: This work will define the specific epigenetic and transcriptional landscape that constitutes a 'molecular scar' of addiction in a key neuronal population. It will shift the therapeutic focus from targeting transient signaling pathways to reversing stable maladaptive gene programs, identifying novel targets for treating protracted withdrawal and preventing relapse.
proposals.novelty: This directly challenges the prevailing focus on sustained neuroadaptations by proposing that a transient signal creates a persistent disease state through epigenetic memory, a conceptual shift from addiction neurobiology to developmental epigenetics. It leverages cell-type-specific epigenomics in a behaviorally defined neuronal ensemble, a technically innovative integration.

proposals.title: Dissociating cAMP Signaling Dynamics from Persistent Affective Withdrawal: A Cell-Type-Specific Investigation of ΔFosB and CREB in the Nucleus Accumbens
proposals.motivation: A core tenet of addiction neurobiology is that chronic drug exposure induces long-lasting upregulation of cAMP signaling (e.g., via CREB) in the nucleus accumbens (NAc), driving the negative affective state of withdrawal. However, emerging pre-2026 data suggest cAMP pathway components normalize rapidly after cessation, while behavioral signs of withdrawal persist, challenging this causal model. This discrepancy necessitates a re-examination of the molecular drivers of prolonged withdrawal, focusing on transcription factors like ΔFosB that outlast initial signaling changes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the persistent affective symptoms of withdrawal from cocaine or hormonal (estradiol) withdrawal are sustained not by ongoing cAMP/CREB upregulation, but by the stable accumulation of ΔFosB in specific NAc medium spiny neuron (MSN) subtypes, which orchestrates a distinct, long-lasting transcriptional program.
proposals.method: We will use Cre-dependent viral strategies in D1-Cre and D2-Cre mice to manipulate ΔFosB and CREB activity with high temporal precision. Following chronic cocaine or hormone-simulated pregnancy/withdrawal protocols, we will perform longitudinal in vivo fiber photometry of cAMP biosensors (e.g., cADDis) in the NAc alongside behavioral assays for anhedonia and anxiety. Cell-type-specific neuroproteomics of synaptoneurosomes will be employed to map downstream synaptic protein changes. Finally, we will use translating ribosome affinity purification (TRAP) followed by RNA-seq from D1- and D2-MSNs to define the distinct transcriptional landscapes driven by ΔFosB versus CREB during early and late withdrawal phases.
proposals.contribution: This work will directly test the causal role of cAMP signaling dynamics in withdrawal, potentially decoupling it from long-term symptomatology. It will define the unique and overlapping synaptic proteomic and transcriptomic targets of ΔFosB and CREB in D1 vs. D2 MSNs, providing a mechanistic framework for how transient signaling converts into stable neuroadaptations.
proposals.novelty: The proposal challenges the canonical view by temporally dissociating cAMP signaling from behavior and posits ΔFosB, not sustained CREB activity, as the key perpetuator of withdrawal, integrating models of psychostimulant and hormonal withdrawal through a shared molecular lens in defined NAc cell types.

proposals.title: Network Resilience Analysis of Opioid Withdrawal: Testing the Locus Coeruleus Keystone Hypothesis via Sequential Chemogenetic Inhibition
proposals.motivation: The locus coeruleus (LC) is widely considered the central hub driving the aversive somatic and affective symptoms of opioid withdrawal, largely based on correlative activity and lesion studies. However, this view may oversimplify a distributed neural circuit. Pre-2026 work from our lab and others shows that chronic drug exposure induces persistent molecular adaptations, like ΔFosB, in key nodes such as the nucleus accumbens (NAc), BNST, and CeA, suggesting these regions may also be critical for the withdrawal state. Determining whether the LC is a true keystone driver or a correlated component is essential for developing targeted interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the LC is not the sole, necessary driver of opioid withdrawal, and that other stress- and aversion-related brain regions (e.g., BNST, CeA) can sustain withdrawal behaviors independently, indicating a decentralized, resilient network architecture.
proposals.method: We will use a chemogenetic 'network lesion' approach in opioid-dependent mice. DREADDs (hM4Di) will be virally targeted to LC norepinephrine neurons, and to D1-type medium spiny neurons in the NAc core and projection neurons in the BNST and CeA. Following induction of spontaneous or naloxone-precipitated withdrawal, we will perform sequential, combinatorial inhibition of these regions while quantifying somatic signs, affective behaviors (e.g., conditioned place aversion), and real-time neural activity via fiber photometry (GCaMP) in non-inhibited nodes. Network resilience will be assessed by whether withdrawal persists during LC inhibition when other nodes are active.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a direct, causal test of the LC-centric model of withdrawal, mapping the functional connectivity and redundancy within the withdrawal network. It will identify which circuit nodes are necessary, sufficient, or compensatory, offering a more nuanced framework for understanding withdrawal pathophysiology. The findings could redirect therapeutic strategies toward multiple network targets beyond the LC.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying an ecological 'keystone species' testing framework to neural circuitry and using sequential, multi-node inhibition to dissect network resilience, moving beyond single-region lesion studies. This directly challenges a decades-old central hypothesis in addiction neurobiology.

proposals.title: Cell-Type-Specific Mapping of ΔFosB-Dependent Withdrawal Circuits: Dissociating Locus Coeruleus Activity from Independent Motivational Drivers
proposals.motivation: The locus coeruleus (LC) is a canonical hub for opioid and psychostimulant withdrawal, but withdrawal is a multi-symptom state likely involving distributed circuits. Pre-2026 work, including from our lab, shows that ΔFosB accumulates in nucleus accumbens (NAc) D1 and D2 medium spiny neurons (MSNs) during chronic drug exposure and withdrawal states, suggesting these enduring transcriptional adaptations may drive maladaptive behaviors independently of LC hyperactivity. Determining if these and other FosB-expressing ensembles are primary drivers, rather than secondary correlates of LC activity, is critical for understanding withdrawal's neural basis.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific, ΔFosB-accumulating neuronal populations in the NAc and related limbic regions (e.g., ventral pallidum, bed nucleus of the stria terminalis) generate core affective and motivational withdrawal symptoms through cell-type-specific circuit adaptations that are functionally dissociable from noradrenergic LC activity.
proposals.method: We will use a FosB-TRAP2 mouse line to target ΔFosB-expressing ensembles activated during cocaine withdrawal. Using Cre-dependent chemogenetics (DREADDs) and optogenetics, we will selectively inhibit or activate these ensembles in NAc D1- or D2-MSNs while simultaneously recording LC neuronal activity via fiber photometry of GCaMP signals. Behavioral assays will measure anxiety-like behavior (elevated plus maze) and anhedonia (sucrose preference). Causal contributions will be tested by manipulating FosB+ ensembles during LC silencing (via clonidine or chemogenetics) to see if withdrawal behaviors persist.
proposals.contribution: This work will identify specific, transcriptionally defined cell populations that are necessary and sufficient for generating withdrawal behaviors, moving beyond correlation to causation. It will establish a framework for withdrawal as a multi-circuit disorder, potentially revealing novel, LC-independent therapeutic targets for addiction treatment.
proposals.novelty: While LC's role is well-established, this proposal directly tests the non-obvious idea that ΔFosB-stabilized ensembles in motivation/reward circuits are primary generative nodes for withdrawal, not merely downstream of LC noradrenergic drive, using simultaneous cell-type-specific manipulation and LC recording.

proposals.title: Temporally Specific Inhibition of ΔFosB-Regulated Pathways in D1-MSNs Dissociates Acute from Protracted Cocaine Withdrawal Phenotypes
